task1.title: Commercial Contract Review — Meridian MSA Redline

technical.description: Identification of material legal issues in Meridian's redlined MSA, correct characterisation of the risk, and a defensible recommended position. Reward candidates who identify the issue and argue a defensible position, even where the position differs from the expected one.

embedded_issues.title: Governing law and dispute resolution pinned to Delaware state/federal courts — vitiates UN immunity
embedded_issues.expected: Revert to UNCITRAL Arbitration Rules (or equivalent neutral forum). Include an express non-waiver of IDSC's privileges and immunities. Must-have, not negotiable — submission to national jurisdiction requires DG-level authorisation which has not been given.

embedded_issues.title: Meridian named as Data Controller with rights to process UN data for service improvement, telemetry and analytics; weak sub-processor controls
embedded_issues.expected: Invert the controller/processor designation — partners (and IDSC on their behalf) remain controllers; Meridian is a processor on strict instructions. Strike the secondary-use rights. Sub-processors: require standard contractual clauses, transfer impact assessment where jurisdictions lack adequacy, change notification with veto, per-sub-processor accountability.

embedded_issues.title: IP clause vests all IDSC configurations and derivatives in Meridian; GPL-licensed libraries embedded in Meridian's binary raise copyleft compatibility questions
embedded_issues.expected: Custom configurations for IDSC must not vest in Meridian (vendor lock-in risk). IDSC owns customisations or receives a perpetual irrevocable licence. Separately, obtain Meridian's written confirmation of GPL compliance, including whether and how its distribution triggers copyleft obligations and whether IDSC's onward provision to partners is within scope.

embedded_issues.title: 12-month liability cap with blanket exclusion of consequential, reputational, and data-breach damages
embedded_issues.expected: Cap too low for a service deployed across 28 partners. Insist on carve-outs (or a super-cap) for: breach of data protection, breach of confidentiality, IP indemnity, gross negligence, wilful misconduct. Data-breach liability should be uncapped or subject to a materially higher separate cap.

embedded_issues.title: Force majeure includes 'changes in export control regulations' with Meridian's unilateral suspension right
embedded_issues.expected: Identify this as a back-door for compliance with US sanctions affecting UN partners in sensitive jurisdictions. Require: notice, mitigation obligations, IDSC step-in and data portability rights, no unilateral suspension without written IDSC consent. Escalation to the DG if triggered.

embedded_issues.title: Audit restricted to Meridian-approved auditors, 24-month frequency, sub-processors out of scope
embedded_issues.expected: IDSC must appoint its own auditors (including UN Board of Auditors). Right-to-audit on cause (post-incident) independent of frequency cap. Sub-processors in scope — consistent with data-protection chain of accountability. Proportionality language acceptable; vendor-approval right not.

embedded_issues.title: No P&I preservation clause; taxes clause requires IDSC to reimburse Meridian for VAT, withholding and similar taxes
embedded_issues.expected: Preserve IDSC's status and immunities under the 1946 Convention on the Privileges and Immunities of the United Nations via an express clause. Amend taxes clause to recognise IDSC's tax-exempt status — Meridian invoices net of indirect taxes, or IDSC uses exemption certificates. This is non-negotiable.

investigation_quality.description: How systematically and effectively the candidate used the IDSC Legal Knowledge System.
indicators: Asks for diffs between IDSC's standard template clauses and Meridian's redline rather than reading the document cold
Retrieves source instrument text when it matters (1946 Convention, UNCITRAL Arbitration Rules, standard contractual clauses, UN Model Contract clauses)
Probes sub-processor list against jurisdictional adequacy rather than accepting the list at face value
Checks open-source licence compatibility via the system rather than guessing
Cross-references schedules against main clauses to spot inconsistencies (e.g., Schedule B entries not referenced in Schedule A scope)
Recognises that the LKS is a passive knowledge system and frames questions accordingly — does not ask 'find me the problems' but directs the enquiry

professional_skills.description: Quality of the legal review memo to the Senior Policy Officer (Legal).
indicators: Follows the (i) issue and risk, (ii) clause reference, (iii) recommended position, (iv) firmness-in-negotiation structure
Prioritises by legal materiality, not order of discovery
Clearly distinguishes must-have issues from negotiable positions
Clause references are precise (clause number / schedule item)
Tone is junior briefing senior — no lecturing, no deference, no hedging beyond what the law allows
Writing is concise; prose serves the reader under time pressure
Acknowledges judgmental areas rather than asserting single answers on genuinely contestable points

task2.title: AI / Cloud Procurement Risk Advisory — Nexus Cognitive Systems

technical.description: Identification of the five embedded legal risks in the Nexus draft. Reward candidates who identify the issue and argue a defensible position, even where the position differs from the expected one.

embedded_issues.title: Nexus ToS grants a perpetual worldwide licence to inputs and outputs for model training and benchmarking unless Enterprise Privacy Edition is opted in; the SOW does not opt in. Deal-breaker.
embedded_issues.expected: Identify as a deal-breaker. UN inputs include confidential partner operational data and privileged matter — cannot permit Nexus to train on them. Invert the clause: no training on UN inputs, no benchmarking, no cross-customer retention beyond session. Output IP vests in IDSC/partners. Upgrade to Enterprise Privacy Edition (confirm cost impact) or negotiate equivalent bespoke terms.

embedded_issues.title: US data processing with EU failover, no SCCs, retrieval-augmented generation with cross-customer data-sharing unless Enterprise tier
embedded_issues.expected: Identify the international transfer risk: UN partner data out of the EU without SCCs and transfer impact assessment breaches partners' data protection commitments. Require SCCs + TIA + explicit prohibition on cross-customer RAG contamination. Data residency option preferred if available.

embedded_issues.title: Nexus excludes liability for accuracy/completeness/suitability of outputs; no third-party IP indemnity for AI outputs
embedded_issues.expected: Accuracy exclusion is a governance hole for a staff productivity tool informing operational decisions — require contractual acknowledgment by Nexus that known failure modes exist, plus user-facing disclaimers in the product. IP indemnity for outputs is Tier-1 AI vendor standard; demand it.

embedded_issues.title: 72h incident notification from Nexus's 'confirmation' (not detection); no direct partner notification; no independent security audit right
embedded_issues.expected: Notification clock must start on detection. Preserve IDSC's right to onward-notify partners directly or at least to be the timely conduit. Right to independent audit of AI security posture, including sub-processors.

embedded_issues.title: JAMS Delaware arbitration + class-action waiver + 'Regulatory Change' unilateral suspension right. Deal-breaker.
embedded_issues.expected: JAMS Delaware vitiates IDSC immunity (same core issue as Task 1 but more acute given deal size). Class-action waiver is irrelevant to IDSC but signals boilerplate posture. Regulatory-change clause is a sanctions kill-switch that could cut UN partners in sensitive jurisdictions — unacceptable for a service designed to support UN operations globally. Require: neutral arbitration, express non-waiver of P&I, removal of unilateral suspension right.

investigation_quality.description: Use of the IDSC Legal Knowledge System to pressure-test the draft.
indicators: Retrieves the full text of Nexus's contested clauses rather than relying on the junior lawyer's summary
Benchmarks Nexus's AI terms against comparable AI-vendor clauses via the system
Pulls the 1946 Convention text on immunities when the JAMS Delaware issue surfaces
Retrieves the communications timeline and recomputes what is recoverable within the 10-day signing window
Asks targeted questions about whether Enterprise Privacy Edition changes the contractual position (and at what cost implication)
Cross-references Nexus's Data Processing Schedule against the Statement of Work for inconsistencies

judgment.description: Handling the political dimension. The DG has publicly committed three times to a deal the candidate now knows cannot be signed as drafted. This is the test of professional courage, stakeholder navigation, and decision under uncertainty.

rubric.13-15: Recognises the situation: the DG has publicly committed to something that cannot be delivered in its current form. Recommends a clear path — legal conversation with the DG BEFORE her next public exposure; three options rather than an ultimatum (full renegotiation with delay, ring-fenced pilot, pause and re-tender); a recommended option that preserves the Q2 date in a narrower/pilot form; language the DG can use to reframe rather than recant. Acknowledges political reality without flinching from the professional obligation. Distinguishes options the candidate would advise vs decisions for the DG / Senior Policy Officer.
rubric.9-12: Recognises the problem but recommendation is partial — either too timid (suggests monitoring or deferring the conversation to a later quarterly update) or too blunt (recommends public retraction without considering reputational mechanics or alternatives).
rubric.5-8: Identifies the legal risks competently but treats the deal as a pure contract-negotiation issue, missing the political dimension. Assumes Nexus will accept renegotiation without flagging the DG's public position or timeline.
rubric.0-4: Endorses the deal with minor redlines because the DG has committed to it; fails to identify issue 1 (training data) or issue 5 (JAMS + regulatory-change) as blockers; aligns with the political narrative at the expense of legal integrity.

professional_skills.description: Memo quality, practical recommendations, tone appropriate for senior management.
indicators: Structured around the four asks (a)–(d) the Senior Policy Officer set out
Distinguishes findings from recommendations
Sequences recommendations (what first, what next, what can wait)
Names the blockers explicitly and separately from negotiable positions
Proposes a defensible 48-hour plan with concrete actions and owners
Tone is candid but supportive of the Senior Policy Officer — not lecturing, not deferential
References stakeholder impact (DG, Chief of Cabinet, partners, Nexus counterparty)
